s1-001.trainerCadence:
Welcome—I'm really glad you're here. Today is the foundation for everything else we'll do in Launch, so I want to start by naming three landmarks we're going to walk through together: why partnership development matters, the biblical foundation underneath it, and the philosophy and attitude that will shape how you show up.

Before we ever talk tactics or strategy, we need to settle something deeper—our theology, our mindset, and our identity. Let these three themes on the slide be our roadmap for the next few minutes.
s1-001.trainerTransition: When you're ready, let's go to the next slide and round out the roadmap for this session.
s1-001.trainerScriptNotes:
- Warm, settled open; let the room arrive before you teach.
- Land all three roadmap bullets before advancing—don't skip ahead to technique.
- Watch for tension when you say "support"; note who looks relieved vs guarded.

s1-001b.trainerCadence: Later on we're going to spend real time with limiting beliefs and with the FREEDOM pathway you see listed here. I'm not going to unpack those deeply in this moment—I just want you to see them coming so nothing feels like it dropped out of nowhere.
s1-001b.trainerTransition: Let's step into the reframe that's going to anchor our whole conversation.
s1-001b.trainerScriptNotes:
- Tease only; resist teaching FREEDOM or beliefs frameworks here.
- Tone: orienting, not dumping content.
- Keep this beat short—energy should move toward the big reframe.

s1-002.trainerCadence:
I want to slow us down here, because this slide is a reset for how we think and feel about partnership development. Before we say what it is, let's name honestly what it is not—it's not fundraising in the usual sense, it's not begging, and it's not selling yourself.

Let those land. We're clearing the ground so the positive definition can actually be heard.
s1-002.trainerTransition: When that feels grounded, let's go on and name what this is in a positive, mission-shaped way.
s1-002.trainerScriptNotes:
- This is an emotional and theological hinge—unhurried pace.
- Name the "nots" clearly without shaming anyone's past experience.
- Let the slide breathe; don't rush to fix discomfort.

s1-002b.trainerCadence:
Here's the other side of that reframe: you're not asking people to fund you in some abstract sense—you're inviting them into what God is already doing. Partnership development, at its best, sounds like invitation, not invoice.

If it helps, I might say that line twice—sometimes we have to hear the good news more than once to believe it.
s1-002b.trainerTransition: From here, we're going to ask the question almost everyone carries underneath the surface: is this way of living even biblical?
s1-002b.trainerScriptNotes:
- Let the positive frame land after the negations—sequence matters.
- Optional: repeat the anchor emphasis line for retention.
- Invite a genuine question after, not a forced "amen."

s1-003.trainerCadence:
As we turn this corner, I want us to be clear about our starting point. We're not beginning with opinions or pragmatics—we're beginning with Scripture. We'll let the text speak first.

Then—and only then—we'll bring our resistance into the light and examine it next to what God has actually said.
s1-003.trainerTransition: Next we're going to give you time in small groups with assigned passages—so go ahead and open your rooms when you're ready.
s1-003.trainerScriptNotes:
- Frame this as humility before the text, not winning an argument.
- Signal safety for honest questions later.
- Clear handoff to breakout logistics on the next slide.

s1-003a.trainerCadence:
You're going into breakout rooms—typically triads. Each room gets a passage theme; use the facilitator panel to assign themes, and if you have fewer rooms than themes, you can combine thoughtfully. Keep the main slide visible so everyone can see the five steps we're asking them to follow.

The deeper study lives in the workbook, but the rhythm is on the screen. You'll want about ten to twelve minutes, then we'll close rooms and debrief together before we teach the biblical pattern overview.
s1-003a.trainerTransition: When time's up, bring everyone back—then we'll debrief with "What Did You See?" before we move on.
s1-003a.trainerScriptNotes:
- Assign themes clearly; confusion here wastes breakout time.
- Keep slide up; watch for groups staring at the wrong screen.
- 30-second warning before closing rooms (see pause cue).
- After debrief slide, plan to teach The Biblical Pattern.

s1-003d.trainerCadence:
Welcome back. Before we rush forward, I want us to process together in the main room—briefly, honestly, and without performance. Walk through what's on the slide: what stood out, what challenged you, what became clearer, what surprised you.

Keep shares concise—we're listening for themes and tensions, not long teaching moments from each person.
s1-003d.trainerTransition: When you've honored the room's voice, let's step into the overview slide on the broad biblical pattern.
s1-003d.trainerScriptNotes:
- Main-room debrief ~5–7 min; protect time for pattern teaching.
- Listen for recurring words—mirror them back.
- Honor both conviction and honest uncertainty.

s1-004.trainerCadence:
Let's zoom out for a moment and name a pattern you see across Scripture—not an exception, but a recurring shape. God uses people. Ministry gets provided for. Workers receive support.

I'm not asking you to memorize a system yet—I'm asking you to feel the weight of how ordinary this pattern actually is in God's story.
s1-004.trainerTransition: On the next slide we'll complete the pattern—how partnership and mission fit into the same picture.
s1-004.trainerScriptNotes:
- Overview before proof texts—don't over-nuance yet.
- Use inclusive language; avoid sounding defensive.
- Let the three bullets feel like one movement.

s1-004b.trainerCadence:
Here's the rest of that same pattern: partners share in fruit, and provision serves mission—not private comfort, but missional purpose.

What I want you to feel is that this isn't a weird side lane in the Bible. It's woven into how God advances his work through his people.
s1-004b.trainerTransition: Now let's get concrete with some New Testament texts where you can see this up close.
s1-004b.trainerScriptNotes:
- Close the overview with confidence, not apology.
- Optional prompt: "What stands out about God's usual pattern of provision?"
- Bridge explicitly to specific passages next.

s1-005.trainerCadence:
Look at Luke with me—Jesus' ministry wasn't disconnected from the generosity of people who partnered in what he was doing. Women and others helped support his ministry; resources actually fueled mission; partnership advanced the work.

I don't want to overcomplicate the text. Let it say what it says, plainly: even Jesus' ministry involved supported partnership.
s1-005.trainerTransition: Next we'll see Paul teaching this pattern with even more explicit language.
s1-005.trainerScriptNotes:
- Let the passage's simplicity land; resist cleverness.
- If you use scripture on-slide, let eyes find it before you talk over it.
- Watch for surprise—many assume Jesus' ministry was purely "self-contained."

s1-006.trainerCadence:
Paul isn't shy about this. In Corinthians and Philippians you see one consistent pattern: those who sow spiritual things may share in material partnership—and that partnership has the flavor of shared fruit, not shameful charity.

Receiving support for ministry, in Paul's logic, can be appropriate. It's rooted in gospel partnership, not in personal worthiness games.
s1-006.trainerTransition: Now someone always asks about tentmaking—so let's name that honestly, in proportion to Scripture.
s1-006.trainerScriptNotes:
- Draw out partnership language vs donation language.
- Connect Philippians 4 to shared fruit without sidetracking.
- Optional prompt about Paul's vocabulary.
- Acknowledge tentmaking is coming so the room feels heard.

s1-007.trainerCadence:
Let's talk about tentmaking without making it a weapon. Sometimes it's appropriate; it's not always required; Paul sometimes worked with his hands—and none of that erases the legitimacy of supported ministry.

A lot of us were taught, implicitly, that self-support is more noble. I want to gently challenge that assumption before we clean it up on the next beat.
s1-007.trainerTransition: Let's go one slide further and name how Paul also received support—and how mission sets the method.
s1-007.trainerScriptNotes:
- Reduce false guilt; many carry shame here.
- Validate tentmaking without elevating it as the only holy path.
- Watch heads nodding—some need permission; others need correction of caricature.

s1-007b.trainerCadence:
Scripture gives us more than one pattern, but it does not shame faithful, supported ministry. At the end of the day, mission helps determine method—context matters, conscience matters, and God's leading matters.

What I want you to carry is proportion: tentmaking can be a tool, but it's not the universal biblical standard for every worker in every season.
s1-007b.trainerTransition: From here we're going to shift from biblical basis to what's happening inside us when we resist these truths.
s1-007b.trainerScriptNotes:
- Land "mission determines method" as a memorable line.
- Optional prompt: why supported ministry can feel "less noble."
- Emotional temperature may rise—keep tone pastoral.

s1-008.trainerCadence:
You've heard a lot of Scripture in a short window—so I want to give you space to process honestly. In your pairs, work through what's on the slide: what still feels uncomfortable or "unbiblical" to you, what challenged your thinking, and what question you're still carrying.

You're not being graded on having it all resolved. Honest questions after Scripture are healthy—not a failure of faith.
s1-008.trainerTransition: When you reconvene, we're going to pivot into philosophy and attitude—how you see God, people, and yourself.
s1-008.trainerScriptNotes:
- ~5 minutes in pairs, then debrief within the 8-minute block.
- Capture recurring tensions verbally or on whiteboard if helpful.
- Watch for shame language—normalize questions.
- Clear time boundary so you can land the pivot.
